question: 2. We consider three vectors drawn from the same initial point \( O \), of lengths \( a, b \), and \( c \), respectively. Let \( E \) be the parallelepiped with vertex \( O \) of which the given vectors are the edges and \( H \) the parallelepiped with vertex \( O \) of which these vectors are the altitudes. Show that the product of the volumes of \( E \) and \( H \) equals ( \( a b c)^{2} \), and generalize the theorem, with proof, to \( n \) dimensions.
solution:
Solution. We proceed at once to the general case. Let \( \mathbf{v}_{1}, \mathbf{v}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_{n} \) be vectors in an \( n \)-dimensional space. To say that these vectors span a parallelepiped \( P \) means that they are linearly independent and that
\[
P=\left\{\Sigma \lambda_{i} \mathbf{v}_{i}: 0 \leq \lambda_{i} \leq 1\right\} .
\]

The volume of the parallelepiped \( P \) is \( |\operatorname{det} A| \) where \( A \) is the \( n \times n \) matrix whose rows are the components of \( \mathbf{v}_{1}, \mathbf{v}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_{n} \) in some Cartesian coordinate system.

Since the \( \mathbf{v} \) 's are linearly independent there are linear functionals \( f_{1} \), \( \ldots, f_{n} \) such that
\[
f_{j}\left(\mathbf{v}_{i}\right)=\delta_{i j}\left\|\mathbf{v}_{i}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( i, j \), where \( \delta_{i j} \) is the Kronecker delta ( \( \delta_{i j}=0 \) if \( i \neq j \), \( \delta_{i i}=1 \) ). Since every linear functional is realizable with an inner product there are vectors \( \mathbf{w}_{1}, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_{n} \) such that
\[
\left(\mathbf{v}_{i}, \mathbf{w}_{i}\right)=\delta_{i j}\left\|_{\mathbf{v}_{i}}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( i, j \). Now the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's are linearly independent, for if \( \Sigma \alpha_{j} \mathbf{w}_{j}=0 \), then
\[
\left(\mathbf{v}_{i}, \Sigma \alpha_{j} \mathbf{w}_{j}\right)=\alpha_{i}\left\|\mathbf{v}_{i}\right\|^{2}=0, \quad i=1,2, \ldots, n
\]
whence \( \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=\cdots=\alpha_{n}=0 \).
Therefore the vectors \( w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n} \) span a parallelepiped \( Q \). The vector \( \mathbf{v}_{i} \) is the altitude of \( Q \) on the face spanned by all the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's except \( \mathbf{w}_{i} \), since it is perpendicular to that face, and the projection of \( \mathbf{w}_{i} \) in the direction of \( \mathbf{v}_{i} \) has length \( \left\|\mathbf{v}_{i}\right\| \) by (1).

Let \( B \) be the \( n \times n \) matrix whose rows are \( \mathrm{w}_{1}, \mathrm{w}_{2}, \ldots, \mathrm{w}_{n} \). Then \( \operatorname{vol} Q=|\operatorname{det} B|=\left|\operatorname{det} B^{T}\right| \), where \( B^{T} \) is the transpose of \( B \).

Now equation (1) shows that \( A B^{T} \) is the diagonal matrix
\[
\operatorname{diag}\left(\left\|\mathbf{v}_{1}\right\|^{2},\left\|\mathbf{v}_{2}\right\|^{2}, \ldots,\left\|\mathbf{v}_{n}\right\|^{2}\right)
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\operatorname{vol} P)(\operatorname{vol} Q) & =|\operatorname{det} A| \cdot\left|\operatorname{det} B^{T}\right|=\left|\operatorname{det} A B^{T}\right| \\
& =\left\|\mathbf{v}_{1}\right\|^{2} \cdot\left\|\mathbf{v}_{2}\right\|^{2} \cdots \cdot\left\|\mathbf{v}_{n}\right\|^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In the three-dimensional case, the problem calls the parallelepipeds \( E \) and \( H \) instead of \( P \) and \( Q \), and, since \( \left\|\mathrm{v}_{1}\right\|=a,\left\|\mathrm{v}_{2}\right\|=b \), and \( \left\|\mathrm{v}_{3}\right\|=c \), the formula above is the required result for the three-dimensional case.

descriptive_long.question: 2. We consider three vectors drawn from the same initial point \( originpoint \), of lengths \( lengtha, lengthb \), and \( lengthc \), respectively. Let \( parallelepipede \) be the parallelepiped with vertex \( originpoint \) of which the given vectors are the edges and \( parallelepipedh \) the parallelepiped with vertex \( originpoint \) of which these vectors are the altitudes. Show that the product of the volumes of \( parallelepipede \) and \( parallelepipedh \) equals \( ( lengtha \; lengthb \; lengthc )^{2} \), and generalize the theorem, with proof, to \( dimensionn \) dimensions.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We proceed at once to the general case. Let \( \mathbf{vectorone}, \mathbf{vectortwo}, \ldots, \mathbf{vectorn} \) be vectors in an \( dimensionn \)-dimensional space. To say that these vectors span a parallelepiped \( parallelepipedp \) means that they are linearly independent and that
\[
parallelepipedp = \{ \Sigma \lambda_{indexi} \, \mathbf{vectori} : 0 \le \lambda_{indexi} \le 1 \} .
\]

The volume of the parallelepiped \( parallelepipedp \) is \( |\operatorname{det} matrixa| \) where \( matrixa \) is the \( dimensionn \times dimensionn \) matrix whose rows are the components of \( \mathbf{vectorone}, \mathbf{vectortwo}, \ldots, \mathbf{vectorn} \) in some Cartesian coordinate system.

Since the \( \mathbf{vectori} \)'s are linearly independent there are linear functionals \( functionalj \), \ldots , \( functionalj \) such that
\[
functionalj(\mathbf{vectori}) = kroneckerdelta \, \| \mathbf{vectori} \|^{2}
\]
for all \( indexi, indexj \), where \( kroneckerdelta \) is the Kronecker delta (\( kroneckerdelta = 0 \) if \( indexi \ne indexj \), \( kroneckerdelta = 1 \)). Because every linear functional can be represented by an inner product, there are vectors \( \mathbf{dualvectorone}, \ldots, \mathbf{dualvectorn} \) such that
\[
( \mathbf{vectori}, \mathbf{dualvectori} ) = kroneckerdelta \, \| \mathbf{vectori} \|^{2}
\]
for all \( indexi, indexj \). The vectors \( \mathbf{dualvectori} \) are linearly independent, for if \( \Sigma \alpha_{indexj} \mathbf{dualvectorj} = 0 \), then
\[
( \mathbf{vectori}, \Sigma \alpha_{indexj} \mathbf{dualvectorj} ) = \alpha_{indexi} \, \| \mathbf{vectori} \|^{2} = 0,\qquad indexi = 1,2, \ldots , dimensionn,
\]
whence \( \alpha_{1} = \alpha_{2} = \cdots = \alpha_{dimensionn} = 0 \).
Therefore the vectors \( dualvectorone, dualvectortwo, \ldots , dualvectorn \) span a parallelepiped \( parallelepipedq \).  The vector \( \mathbf{vectori} \) is the altitude of \( parallelepipedq \) on the face spanned by all the \( \mathbf{dualvectori} \)'s except \( \mathbf{dualvectori} \), since it is perpendicular to that face, and the projection of \( \mathbf{dualvectori} \) in the direction of \( \mathbf{vectori} \) has length \( \| \mathbf{vectori} \| \).

Let \( matrixb \) be the \( dimensionn \times dimensionn \) matrix whose rows are \( \mathbf{dualvectorone}, \mathbf{dualvectortwo}, \ldots, \mathbf{dualvectorn} \). Then \( \operatorname{vol} parallelepipedq = |\operatorname{det} matrixb| = |\operatorname{det} matrixb^{T}| \), where \( matrixb^{T} \) is the transpose of \( matrixb \).

Equation (1) shows that \( matrixa \, matrixb^{T} \) is the diagonal matrix
\[
\operatorname{diag}( \| \mathbf{vectorone} \|^{2}, \| \mathbf{vectortwo} \|^{2}, \ldots , \| \mathbf{vectorn} \|^{2} ).
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
( \operatorname{vol} parallelepipedp )( \operatorname{vol} parallelepipedq )
&= |\operatorname{det} matrixa| \cdot |\operatorname{det} matrixb^{T}| = |\operatorname{det}( matrixa \, matrixb^{T} )| \\ 
&= \| \mathbf{vectorone} \|^{2} \cdot \| \mathbf{vectortwo} \|^{2} \cdots \| \mathbf{vectorn} \|^{2}.
\end{aligned}
\]

In the three-dimensional case, the problem labels the parallelepipeds \( parallelepipede \) and \( parallelepipedh \) instead of \( parallelepipedp \) and \( parallelepipedq \). Since \( \| \mathbf{vectorone} \| = lengtha, \| \mathbf{vectortwo} \| = lengthb \), and \( \| \mathbf{vectorthree} \| = lengthc \), the above formula gives the required result for three dimensions.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 2. We consider three vectors drawn from the same initial point \( blueberry \), of lengths \( lemonade, toothbrush \), and \( horseshoe \), respectively. Let \( marshmallow \) be the parallelepiped with vertex \( blueberry \) of which the given vectors are the edges and \( tortoise \) the parallelepiped with vertex \( blueberry \) of which these vectors are the altitudes. Show that the product of the volumes of \( marshmallow \) and \( tortoise \) equals ( \( lemonade toothbrush horseshoe)^{2} \), and generalize the theorem, with proof, to \( dandelion \) dimensions.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We proceed at once to the general case. Let \( \mathbf{pineapple}, \mathbf{hummingbird}, \ldots, \mathbf{blacksmith} \) be vectors in an \( dandelion \)-dimensional space. To say that these vectors span a parallelepiped \( rainforest \) means that they are linearly independent and that
\[
rainforest=\left\{\Sigma \lambda_{peppermint} \mathbf{sailboat}: 0 \leq \lambda_{peppermint} \leq 1ight\} .
\]
The volume of the parallelepiped \( rainforest \) is \( |\operatorname{det} chandelier| \) where \( chandelier \) is the \( dandelion 	imes dandelion \) matrix whose rows are the components of \( \mathbf{pineapple}, \mathbf{hummingbird}, \ldots, \mathbf{blacksmith} \) in some Cartesian coordinate system.

Since the \( \mathbf{v} \) 's are linearly independent there are linear functionals \( f_{1}, \ldots, f_{dandelion} \) such that
\[
f_{scarecrow}\left(\mathbf{sailboat}ight)=moonlight\left\|\mathbf{sailboat}ight\|^{2}
\]for all \( peppermint, scarecrow \), where \( moonlight \) is the Kronecker delta ( \( moonlight=0 \) if \( peppermint 
eq scarecrow \), \( moonlight=1 \) ). Since every linear functional is realizable with an inner product there are vectors \( \mathbf{grasshopper}, \ldots, \mathbf{kingfisher} \) such that
\[
\left(\mathbf{sailboat}, \mathbf{buttercup}ight)=moonlight\left\|\mathbf{sailboat}ight\|^{2}
\]for all \( peppermint, scarecrow \). Now the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's are linearly independent, for if \( \Sigma \alpha_{scarecrow} \mathbf{w}_{scarecrow}=0 \), then
\[
\left(\mathbf{sailboat}, \Sigma \alpha_{scarecrow} \mathbf{w}_{scarecrow}ight)=\alpha_{peppermint}\left\|\mathbf{sailboat}ight\|^{2}=0, \quad peppermint=1,2, \ldots, dandelion
\]whence \( \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=\cdots=\alpha_{dandelion}=0 \). Therefore the vectors grasshopper, partridge, \ldots, kingfisher span a parallelepiped \( woodpecker \). The vector \( \mathbf{sailboat} \) is the altitude of \( woodpecker \) on the face spanned by all the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's except \( \mathbf{buttercup} \), since it is perpendicular to that face, and the projection of \( \mathbf{buttercup} \) in the direction of \( \mathbf{sailboat} \) has length \( \left\|\mathbf{sailboat}ight\| \) by (1).

Let \( stormcloud \) be the \( dandelion 	imes dandelion \) matrix whose rows are \( \mathrm{grasshopper}, \mathrm{partridge}, \ldots, \mathrm{kingfisher} \). Then \( \operatorname{vol} woodpecker=|\operatorname{det} stormcloud|=\left|\operatorname{det} stormcloud^{T}ight| \), where \( stormcloud^{T} \) is the transpose of \( stormcloud \).

Now equation (1) shows that \( chandelier\,stormcloud^{T} \) is the diagonal matrix
\[
\operatorname{diag}\left(\left\|\mathbf{pineapple}ight\|^{2},\left\|\mathbf{hummingbird}ight\|^{2}, \ldots, \left\|\mathbf{blacksmith}ight\|^{2}ight)
\]
Hence
\[
 egin{aligned}
(\operatorname{vol} rainforest)(\operatorname{vol} woodpecker) & =|\operatorname{det} chandelier| \cdot \left|\operatorname{det} stormcloud^{T}ight| = \left|\operatorname{det} (chandelier\,stormcloud^{T})ight| \ & =\left\|\mathbf{pineapple}ight\|^{2} \cdot \left\|\mathbf{hummingbird}ight\|^{2} \cdots \cdot \left\|\mathbf{blacksmith}ight\|^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]
In the three-dimensional case, the problem calls the parallelepipeds \( marshmallow \) and \( tortoise \) instead of \( rainforest \) and \( woodpecker \), and, since \( \left\|\mathrm{pineapple}ight\|=lemonade, \left\|\mathrm{hummingbird}ight\|=toothbrush \), and \( \left\|\mathrm{whirligig}ight\|=horseshoe \), the formula above is the required result for the three-dimensional case.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 2. We consider three vectors drawn from the same initial point \( abysspoint \), of lengths \( shortside, briefside \), and \( smallside \), respectively. Let \( emptybox \) be the parallelepiped with vertex \( abysspoint \) of which the given vectors are the edges and \( hollowshell \) the parallelepiped with vertex \( abysspoint \) of which these vectors are the altitudes. Show that the product of the volumes of \( emptybox \) and \( hollowshell \) equals ( \( shortside briefside smallside)^{2} \), and generalize the theorem, with proof, to \( singledim \) dimensions.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We proceed at once to the general case. Let \( \mathbf{stillvector}_{1}, \mathbf{calmvector}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{inertvector}_{singledim} \) be vectors in an \( singledim \)-dimensional space. To say that these vectors span a parallelepiped \( vacuumshape \) means that they are linearly independent and that
\[
vacuumshape=\left\{\Sigma \lambda_{constantix} \mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}: 0 \leq \lambda_{constantix} \leq 1\right\} .
\]

The volume of the parallelepiped \( vacuumshape \) is \( |\operatorname{det} antiarray| \) where \( antiarray \) is the \( singledim \times singledim \) matrix whose rows are the components of \( \mathbf{stillvector}_{1}, \mathbf{calmvector}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{inertvector}_{singledim} \) in some Cartesian coordinate system.

Since the \( \mathbf{v} \) 's are linearly independent there are linear functionals \( silentfunction_{1}, \ldots, silentfunction_{singledim} \) such that
\[
silentfunction_{unchanging}\left(\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}\right)=nonkronecker_{constantix\ unchanging}\left\|\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( constantix, unchanging \), where \( nonkronecker_{constantix\ unchanging} \) is the Kronecker delta ( \( nonkronecker_{constantix\ unchanging}=0 \) if \( constantix \neq unchanging \), \( nonkronecker_{constantix\ constantix}=1 \) ). Since every linear functional is realizable with an inner product there are vectors \( \mathbf{quietvector}_{1}, \ldots, \mathbf{nullvector}_{singledim} \) such that
\[
\left(\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}, \mathbf{numbvector}_{constantix}\right)=nonkronecker_{constantix\ unchanging}\left\|\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( constantix, unchanging \). Now the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's are linearly independent, for if \( \Sigma \alpha_{unchanging} \mathbf{numbvector}_{unchanging}=0 \), then
\[
\left(\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}, \Sigma \alpha_{unchanging} \mathbf{numbvector}_{unchanging}\right)=\alpha_{constantix}\left\|\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}\right\|^{2}=0, \quad constantix=1,2, \ldots, singledim
\]
whence \( \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=\cdots=\alpha_{singledim}=0 \).
Therefore the vectors \( quietvector_{1}, quietvector_{2}, \ldots, quietvector_{singledim} \) span a parallelepiped \( voidsolid \). The vector \( \mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix} \) is the altitude of \( voidsolid \) on the face spanned by all the \( \mathbf{numbvector} \) 's except \( \mathbf{numbvector}_{constantix} \), since it is perpendicular to that face, and the projection of \( \mathbf{numbvector}_{constantix} \) in the direction of \( \mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix} \) has length \( \left\|\mathbf{idlevector}_{constantix}\right\| \) by (1).

Let \( blankmatrix \) be the \( singledim \times singledim \) matrix whose rows are \( \mathrm{quietvector}_{1}, \mathrm{mutevector}_{2}, \ldots, \mathrm{nullvector}_{singledim} \). Then \( \operatorname{vol} voidsolid=|\operatorname{det} blankmatrix|=\left|\operatorname{det} blankmatrix^{T}\right| \), where \( blankmatrix^{T} \) is the transpose of \( blankmatrix \).

Now equation (1) shows that \( antiarray blankmatrix^{T} \) is the diagonal matrix
\[
\operatorname{diag}\left(\left\|\mathbf{stillvector}_{1}\right\|^{2},\left\|\mathbf{calmvector}_{2}\right\|^{2}, \ldots,\left\|\mathbf{inertvector}_{singledim}\right\|^{2}\right)
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\operatorname{vol} vacuumshape)(\operatorname{vol} voidsolid) & =|\operatorname{det} antiarray| \cdot\left|\operatorname{det} blankmatrix^{T}\right|=\left|\operatorname{det} antiarray blankmatrix^{T}\right| \\
& =\left\|\mathbf{stillvector}_{1}\right\|^{2} \cdot\left\|\mathbf{calmvector}_{2}\right\|^{2} \cdots \cdot\left\|\mathbf{inertvector}_{singledim}\right\|^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In the three-dimensional case, the problem calls the parallelepipeds \( emptybox \) and \( hollowshell \) instead of \( vacuumshape \) and \( voidsolid \), and, since \( \left\|\mathrm{stillvector}_{1}\right\|=shortside,\left\|\mathrm{calmvector}_{2}\right\|=briefside \), and \( \left\|\mathrm{hushvector}_{3}\right\|=smallside \), the formula above is the required result for the three-dimensional case.

garbled_string.question: 2. We consider three vectors drawn from the same initial point \( mavncytq \), of lengths \( cvtebqsl, znofkjla \), and \( wexparcd \), respectively. Let \( jahyxdeo \) be the parallelepiped with vertex \( mavncytq \) of which the given vectors are the edges and \( qopnezal \) the parallelepiped with vertex \( mavncytq \) of which these vectors are the altitudes. Show that the product of the volumes of \( jahyxdeo \) and \( qopnezal \) equals ( \( cvtebqsl znofkjla wexparcd)^{2} \), and generalize the theorem, with proof, to \( lycaspem \) dimensions.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We proceed at once to the general case. Let \( \mathbf{exybdhom}, \mathbf{krqadlen}, \ldots, \mathbf{kwhumelo} \) be vectors in an \( lycaspem \)-dimensional space. To say that these vectors span a parallelepiped \( zlbyrken \) means that they are linearly independent and that
\[
zlbyrken=\left\{\Sigma \lambda_{hrqstlva} \mathbf{bjraxuln}: 0 \leq \lambda_{hrqstlva} \leq 1\right\} .
\]

The volume of the parallelepiped \( zlbyrken \) is \( |\operatorname{det} tpqirfux| \) where \( tpqirfux \) is the \( lycaspem \times lycaspem \) matrix whose rows are the components of \( \mathbf{exybdhom}, \mathbf{krqadlen}, \ldots, \mathbf{kwhumelo} \) in some Cartesian coordinate system.

Since the \( \mathbf{v} \) 's are linearly independent there are linear functionals \( dlygirca_{1} \), \( \ldots, dlygirca_{lycaspem} \) such that
\[
dlygirca_{mpowzcre}\left(\mathbf{bjraxuln}\right)=zlwqmsnf\left\|\mathbf{bjraxuln}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( hrqstlva, mpowzcre \), where \( zlwqmsnf \) is the Kronecker delta ( \( zlwqmsnf=0 \) if \( hrqstlva \neq mpowzcre \), \( zlwqmsnf=1 \) ). Since every linear functional is realizable with an inner product there are vectors \( \mathbf{vchoymsd}, \ldots, \mathbf{swnekplo} \) such that
\[
\left(\mathbf{bjraxuln}, \mathbf{yzqfanid}\right)=zlwqmsnf\left\|_{\mathbf{bjraxuln}}\right\|^{2}
\]
for all \( hrqstlva, mpowzcre \). Now the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's are linearly independent, for if \( \Sigma \alpha_{mpowzcre} \mathbf{yzqfanid}=0 \), then
\[
\left(\mathbf{bjraxuln}, \Sigma \alpha_{mpowzcre} \mathbf{yzqfanid}\right)=\alpha_{hrqstlva}\left\|\mathbf{bjraxuln}\right\|^{2}=0, \quad hrqstlva=1,2, \ldots, lycaspem
\]
whence \( \alpha_{1}=\alpha_{2}=\cdots=\alpha_{lycaspem}=0 \).
Therefore the vectors \( vchoymsd, gtwlepor, \ldots, swnekplo \) span a parallelepiped \( rksvumeq \). The vector \( \mathbf{bjraxuln} \) is the altitude of \( rksvumeq \) on the face spanned by all the \( \mathbf{w} \) 's except \( \mathbf{yzqfanid} \), since it is perpendicular to that face, and the projection of \( \mathbf{yzqfanid} \) in the direction of \( \mathbf{bjraxuln} \) has length \( \left\|\mathbf{bjraxuln}\right\| \) by (1).

Let \( xevmanor \) be the \( lycaspem \times lycaspem \) matrix whose rows are \( \mathrm{vchoymsd}, \mathrm{gtwlepor}, \ldots, \mathrm{swnekplo} \). Then \( \operatorname{vol} rksvumeq=|\operatorname{det} xevmanor|=\left|\operatorname{det} xevmanor^{T}\right| \), where \( xevmanor^{T} \) is the transpose of \( xevmanor \).

Now equation (1) shows that \( tpqirfux xevmanor^{T} \) is the diagonal matrix
\[
\operatorname{diag}\left(\left\|\mathbf{exybdhom}\right\|^{2},\left\|\mathbf{krqadlen}\right\|^{2}, \ldots,\left\|\mathbf{kwhumelo}\right\|^{2}\right)
\]

Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\operatorname{vol} zlbyrken)(\operatorname{vol} rksvumeq) & =|\operatorname{det} tpqirfux| \cdot\left|\operatorname{det} xevmanor^{T}\right|=\left|\operatorname{det} tpqirfux xevmanor^{T}\right| \\
& =\left\|\mathbf{exybdhom}\right\|^{2} \cdot\left\|\mathbf{krqadlen}\right\|^{2} \cdots \cdot\left\|\mathbf{kwhumelo}\right\|^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

In the three-dimensional case, the problem calls the parallelepipeds \( jahyxdeo \) and \( qopnezal \) instead of \( zlbyrken \) and \( rksvumeq \), and, since \( \left\|\mathrm{exybdhom}\right\|=cvtebqsl,\left\|\mathrm{krqadlen}\right\|=znofkjla \), and \( \left\|\mathrm{phormdaz}\right\|=wexparcd \), the formula above is the required result for the three-dimensional case.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix once and for all  

* a real Euclidean vector space (V,\langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle ) of dimension n \geq  2,  
* the origin O \in  V, and  
* a positively oriented orthonormal basis E = (e_1,\ldots ,e_n).

Row convention.  
For an ordered basis (frame)  
  F = (v_1,\ldots ,v_n)  (v_i\neq 0)  
write the vectors as ROWS of the matrix  
  A = Mat_E(F) \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}),  A = (v_1^T;\ldots ;v_n^T).  

Its row Gram matrix is G := A A^T, and the squared edge-lengths form the diagonal matrix  
  D := diag(\ell _1^2,\ldots ,\ell _n^2) (with \ell _i := \|v_i\|>0).

Definition (altitude operator).  
Given A \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}) let Alt(A)=:B be the unique matrix whose i-th ROW w_i^T satisfies  
 (1) w_i \bot  span{v_j : j\neq i},  (2) \langle v_i,w_i\rangle  = \ell _i^2.

(The w_i are the altitudes from O of the parallelotope with edges v_i.)

Problems.

(a)  Prove the matrix identity Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  

(b)  Denoting by K(F) the edge parallelotope with edges v_i and by L(F) the altitude parallelotope with edges Alt(F), show  
  Vol_n(K(F))\cdot Vol_n(L(F)) = (\ell _1\ell _2\cdots \ell _n)^2   (remember that Vol_n(P)=|det Mat_E(P)|).  

(c)  Put W:=Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  For m_i:=\|w_i\| set E:=diag(m_1^2,\ldots ,m_n^2).

(i)  Show Alt^2(A)=E W^{-T}=S(A)\cdot A with  
  S(A):=E D^{-1}=diag(s_1,\ldots ,s_n), s_i=\ell _i^2\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}>0.

(ii)  Prove det S(A)=\prod  s_i=(\prod  m_i^2)/(\prod  \ell _i^2).  

(iii)  Deduce det Alt^2(A)=det S(A)\cdot det A.

(d)  (Shape-scalar frames)  
Show the equivalence of  
 (i) Alt^2(A)=\lambda (A)\cdot A for some \lambda (A)>0;  
 (ii) S(A)=\lambda (A)\cdot I_n (i.e. s_1=\cdots =s_n);  
 (iii) G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii} is independent of i.  

Moreover \lambda (A)=s_i, hence \lambda (A)^n=det S(A).

(e)  Projectivised frame space.  
Let \mathbb{P}(GL_n):=GL_n/\mathbb{R}^\times  (right action by non-zero scalars) and define the rational map  
  ZAlt : \mathbb{P}(GL_n) \dashrightarrow  \mathbb{P}(GL_n), [A]\mapsto [Alt(A)].  

(i)  Prove that ZAlt is dominant.  

(ii)  Show that a projective class [A] is fixed by ZAlt  
    iff Alt(A)=A  
    iff S(A)=I_n  iff A A^T=D.  
Thus the fixed locus consists precisely of the classes of frames whose vectors are pairwise orthogonal (no restriction on their lengths).

(f)  Specialise to n = 3.  
Classify all ordered bases F for which Alt(F)=R\cdot F with a proper rotation R \in  SO(3).  
Show that this occurs iff the basis vectors are pairwise orthogonal, i.e. A A^T=D, in which case necessarily Alt(F)=F and R=I_3.
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation is taken from the statement: A=(v_1^T;\ldots ;v_n^T), G=AA^T, D=diag(\ell _i^2) and Alt(A)=(w_1^T;\ldots ;w_n^T).

(a)  Altitude matrix.  
Conditions (1)-(2) are equivalent to the single matrix equation  
  A \cdot  Alt(A)^T = D. (*)

Because A \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}), multiplying (*) from the left by A^{-1} gives Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  
Thus Alt is a rational self-map of GL_n.

(b)  Product of volumes.  
Vol_n(K(F)) = |det A| and, by (a), Alt(A)=D A^{-T}; hence  
 det Alt(A)=det(D A^{-T})=(\ell _1^2\cdots \ell _n^2)/(det A).  
Therefore  
 Vol_n(K(F))\cdot Vol_n(L(F))  
 = |det A|\cdot |det Alt(A)|  
 = |det A|\cdot |(\ell _1^2\cdots \ell _n^2)/(det A)|  
 = (\ell _1\ell _2\cdots \ell _n)^2.

(c-i)  Second altitude.  
Set W = Alt(A) = D A^{-T}.  Re-applying (a) to W gives  
 Alt^2(A)=Alt(W)=E W^{-T}.  
For every i  
 w_i^T = \ell _i^2 e_i^T A^{-T},  
so  
 m_i^2 = \ell _i^4\cdot (A^{-T}A^{-1})_{ii}=\ell _i^4\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}.  
Hence  
 S(A):=E D^{-1}=diag(s_i) with s_i=\ell _i^2\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}>0,  
and Alt^2(A)=S(A)\cdot A.

(c-ii)  Since S(A) is diagonal, det S(A)=\prod  s_i=(\prod  m_i^2)/(\prod  \ell _i^2).

(c-iii)  From Alt^2(A)=S(A)A we get det Alt^2(A)=det S(A)\cdot det A.

(d)  Shape-scalar frames.  
(i)\Rightarrow (ii) Comparing Alt^2(A)=\lambda A with Alt^2(A)=S(A)A yields S(A)=\lambda  I_n.  
(ii)\Rightarrow (iii) Equality of the diagonal entries of S(A) means s_i is constant, i.e. G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii} is independent of i.  
(iii)\Rightarrow (i) If G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii}=c then s_i=c and S(A)=c I_n, so Alt^2(A)=c A.  
Finally \lambda (A)=s_i and \lambda (A)^n = det S(A) by (c-ii).

(e)  Projective altitude map.  
(i)  Dominance.  
Compute the differential of Alt at the identity I.  
Let H \in  gl_n(\mathbb{R}) and put A(\varepsilon )=I+\varepsilon H.  Up to O(\varepsilon ^2) one finds  
 Alt(A(\varepsilon )) = I + \varepsilon ( diag(H+H^T) - H^T ) + O(\varepsilon ^2).  
Define L(H):=diag(H+H^T) - H^T.  
Solving L(H)=Y:  
 * If i\neq j, Y_{ij}=-H_{ji}  \Rightarrow  H_{ji}=-Y_{ij}.  
 * If i=j, Y_{ii}=H_{ii}.  
Thus L is bijective, its rank is n^2 and Alt is a submersion at I.  
Consequently its projectivisation ZAlt is dominant.

(ii)  Fixed points.  
If [A] is fixed, Alt(A)=c A for some c\in \mathbb{R}^\times .  Plugging into (*) gives D A^{-T}=c A \Rightarrow  D=c G.  
Taking diagonal entries yields \ell _i^2=c \ell _i^2 \Rightarrow  c=1.  Hence Alt(A)=A and G=D.  
Conversely, G=D \Rightarrow  Alt(A)=A and then S(A)=I_n.  
Thus the three conditions are equivalent, and the fixed locus is exactly the set of projective classes of pairwise-orthogonal frames.

(f)  The three-dimensional case.  
Assume Alt(F)=R F with some R\in SO(3); write A for the frame matrix.  Using (a):  
 D A^{-T}=R A \Rightarrow  D = R G. (1)

(i)  Orthogonality of the basis via Hadamard.  
Both D and G are symmetric positive-definite and have the same diagonal because diag G = diag D = (\ell _1^2,\ell _2^2,\ell _3^2).  
Hadamard's inequality says det G \leq  \prod  G_{ii} = det D, with equality iff G is diagonal.  
Taking determinants in (1) gives det D = det G, so equality holds in Hadamard's inequality and therefore G is diagonal.  But a positive-definite diagonal matrix with the same diagonal as D must equal D; hence G=D and the vectors v_i are pairwise orthogonal.

(ii)  Identification of R.  
With G=D equation (1) becomes D = R D.  Because D is invertible we can multiply by D^{-1} on the right to obtain R = I_3 outright; no further diagonalisation considerations are needed.  Thus Alt(F)=F.

Conversely, if the vectors are pairwise orthogonal (i.e. G=D) then Alt(F)=F, so Alt(F)=R F with R=I_3, which is indeed a proper rotation.

Therefore Alt(F)=R F with R\in SO(3) occurs precisely when the basis vectors are pairwise orthogonal, and in that situation R=I_3.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Additional structures.  The problem introduces the \emph{altitude operator} as a self-map of \(\mathrm{GL}_{n}(\mathbb R)\) and asks for its algebraic properties, creating a bridge between geometry of parallelotopes and linear algebra on the full general linear group.

•  Multiple interacting concepts.  Parts (a)–(e) combine geometric reasoning (orthogonality, volumes), exterior algebra (Gram determinants), linear algebra (matrix inverses and transposes), group actions (projectivisation, rotations) and dynamical aspects (iteration of a nonlinear operator).

•  Deeper theoretical requirements.  One has to manipulate Gram matrices, determinants, involutive rational maps, and perform a fixed–point classification—none of which appears in the original or first kernel variant.

•  Higher technical load.  Whereas the original statement stops after a single volume identity, the enhanced variant demands (i) an explicit matrix formula, (ii) derivation of a second-iterate formula, (iii) projective-geometric interpretation, and (iv) a classification problem in dimension three.

•  Non-trivial proofs.  Parts (c)–(e) cannot be settled by a direct determinant computation alone; they require recognising hidden symmetries, using the polar decomposition of matrices, and engaging with the structure of \(\mathrm{SO}(3)\).

Hence the enhanced kernel variant is significantly more sophisticated and longer than both the original problem and the previous kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix once and for all  

* a real Euclidean vector space (V,\langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle ) of dimension n \geq  2,  
* the origin O \in  V, and  
* a positively oriented orthonormal basis E = (e_1,\ldots ,e_n).

Row convention.  
For an ordered basis (frame)  
  F = (v_1,\ldots ,v_n)  (v_i\neq 0)  
write the vectors as ROWS of the matrix  
  A = Mat_E(F) \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}),  A = (v_1^T;\ldots ;v_n^T).  

Its row Gram matrix is G := A A^T, and the squared edge-lengths form the diagonal matrix  
  D := diag(\ell _1^2,\ldots ,\ell _n^2) (with \ell _i := \|v_i\|>0).

Definition (altitude operator).  
Given A \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}) let Alt(A)=:B be the unique matrix whose i-th ROW w_i^T satisfies  
 (1) w_i \bot  span{v_j : j\neq i},  (2) \langle v_i,w_i\rangle  = \ell _i^2.

(The w_i are the altitudes from O of the parallelotope with edges v_i.)

Problems.

(a)  Prove the matrix identity Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  

(b)  Denoting by K(F) the edge parallelotope with edges v_i and by L(F) the altitude parallelotope with edges Alt(F), show  
  Vol_n(K(F))\cdot Vol_n(L(F)) = (\ell _1\ell _2\cdots \ell _n)^2   (remember that Vol_n(P)=|det Mat_E(P)|).  

(c)  Put W:=Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  For m_i:=\|w_i\| set E:=diag(m_1^2,\ldots ,m_n^2).

(i)  Show Alt^2(A)=E W^{-T}=S(A)\cdot A with  
  S(A):=E D^{-1}=diag(s_1,\ldots ,s_n), s_i=\ell _i^2\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}>0.

(ii)  Prove det S(A)=\prod  s_i=(\prod  m_i^2)/(\prod  \ell _i^2).  

(iii)  Deduce det Alt^2(A)=det S(A)\cdot det A.

(d)  (Shape-scalar frames)  
Show the equivalence of  
 (i) Alt^2(A)=\lambda (A)\cdot A for some \lambda (A)>0;  
 (ii) S(A)=\lambda (A)\cdot I_n (i.e. s_1=\cdots =s_n);  
 (iii) G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii} is independent of i.  

Moreover \lambda (A)=s_i, hence \lambda (A)^n=det S(A).

(e)  Projectivised frame space.  
Let \mathbb{P}(GL_n):=GL_n/\mathbb{R}^\times  (right action by non-zero scalars) and define the rational map  
  ZAlt : \mathbb{P}(GL_n) \dashrightarrow  \mathbb{P}(GL_n), [A]\mapsto [Alt(A)].  

(i)  Prove that ZAlt is dominant.  

(ii)  Show that a projective class [A] is fixed by ZAlt  
    iff Alt(A)=A  
    iff S(A)=I_n  iff A A^T=D.  
Thus the fixed locus consists precisely of the classes of frames whose vectors are pairwise orthogonal (no restriction on their lengths).

(f)  Specialise to n = 3.  
Classify all ordered bases F for which Alt(F)=R\cdot F with a proper rotation R \in  SO(3).  
Show that this occurs iff the basis vectors are pairwise orthogonal, i.e. A A^T=D, in which case necessarily Alt(F)=F and R=I_3.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation is taken from the statement: A=(v_1^T;\ldots ;v_n^T), G=AA^T, D=diag(\ell _i^2) and Alt(A)=(w_1^T;\ldots ;w_n^T).

(a)  Altitude matrix.  
Conditions (1)-(2) are equivalent to the single matrix equation  
  A \cdot  Alt(A)^T = D. (*)

Because A \in  GL_n(\mathbb{R}), multiplying (*) from the left by A^{-1} gives Alt(A)=D A^{-T}.  
Thus Alt is a rational self-map of GL_n.

(b)  Product of volumes.  
Vol_n(K(F)) = |det A| and, by (a), Alt(A)=D A^{-T}; hence  
 det Alt(A)=det(D A^{-T})=(\ell _1^2\cdots \ell _n^2)/(det A).  
Therefore  
 Vol_n(K(F))\cdot Vol_n(L(F))  
 = |det A|\cdot |det Alt(A)|  
 = |det A|\cdot |(\ell _1^2\cdots \ell _n^2)/(det A)|  
 = (\ell _1\ell _2\cdots \ell _n)^2.

(c-i)  Second altitude.  
Set W = Alt(A) = D A^{-T}.  Re-applying (a) to W gives  
 Alt^2(A)=Alt(W)=E W^{-T}.  
For every i  
 w_i^T = \ell _i^2 e_i^T A^{-T},  
so  
 m_i^2 = \ell _i^4\cdot (A^{-T}A^{-1})_{ii}=\ell _i^4\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}.  
Hence  
 S(A):=E D^{-1}=diag(s_i) with s_i=\ell _i^2\cdot (G^{-1})_{ii}>0,  
and Alt^2(A)=S(A)\cdot A.

(c-ii)  Since S(A) is diagonal, det S(A)=\prod  s_i=(\prod  m_i^2)/(\prod  \ell _i^2).

(c-iii)  From Alt^2(A)=S(A)A we get det Alt^2(A)=det S(A)\cdot det A.

(d)  Shape-scalar frames.  
(i)\Rightarrow (ii) Comparing Alt^2(A)=\lambda A with Alt^2(A)=S(A)A yields S(A)=\lambda  I_n.  
(ii)\Rightarrow (iii) Equality of the diagonal entries of S(A) means s_i is constant, i.e. G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii} is independent of i.  
(iii)\Rightarrow (i) If G_{ii}(G^{-1})_{ii}=c then s_i=c and S(A)=c I_n, so Alt^2(A)=c A.  
Finally \lambda (A)=s_i and \lambda (A)^n = det S(A) by (c-ii).

(e)  Projective altitude map.  
(i)  Dominance.  
Compute the differential of Alt at the identity I.  
Let H \in  gl_n(\mathbb{R}) and put A(\varepsilon )=I+\varepsilon H.  Up to O(\varepsilon ^2) one finds  
 Alt(A(\varepsilon )) = I + \varepsilon ( diag(H+H^T) - H^T ) + O(\varepsilon ^2).  
Define L(H):=diag(H+H^T) - H^T.  
Solving L(H)=Y:  
 * If i\neq j, Y_{ij}=-H_{ji}  \Rightarrow  H_{ji}=-Y_{ij}.  
 * If i=j, Y_{ii}=H_{ii}.  
Thus L is bijective, its rank is n^2 and Alt is a submersion at I.  
Consequently its projectivisation ZAlt is dominant.

(ii)  Fixed points.  
If [A] is fixed, Alt(A)=c A for some c\in \mathbb{R}^\times .  Plugging into (*) gives D A^{-T}=c A \Rightarrow  D=c G.  
Taking diagonal entries yields \ell _i^2=c \ell _i^2 \Rightarrow  c=1.  Hence Alt(A)=A and G=D.  
Conversely, G=D \Rightarrow  Alt(A)=A and then S(A)=I_n.  
Thus the three conditions are equivalent, and the fixed locus is exactly the set of projective classes of pairwise-orthogonal frames.

(f)  The three-dimensional case.  
Assume Alt(F)=R F with some R\in SO(3); write A for the frame matrix.  Using (a):  
 D A^{-T}=R A \Rightarrow  D = R G. (1)

(i)  Orthogonality of the basis via Hadamard.  
Both D and G are symmetric positive-definite and have the same diagonal because diag G = diag D = (\ell _1^2,\ell _2^2,\ell _3^2).  
Hadamard's inequality says det G \leq  \prod  G_{ii} = det D, with equality iff G is diagonal.  
Taking determinants in (1) gives det D = det G, so equality holds in Hadamard's inequality and therefore G is diagonal.  But a positive-definite diagonal matrix with the same diagonal as D must equal D; hence G=D and the vectors v_i are pairwise orthogonal.

(ii)  Identification of R.  
With G=D equation (1) becomes D = R D.  Because D is invertible we can multiply by D^{-1} on the right to obtain R = I_3 outright; no further diagonalisation considerations are needed.  Thus Alt(F)=F.

Conversely, if the vectors are pairwise orthogonal (i.e. G=D) then Alt(F)=F, so Alt(F)=R F with R=I_3, which is indeed a proper rotation.

Therefore Alt(F)=R F with R\in SO(3) occurs precisely when the basis vectors are pairwise orthogonal, and in that situation R=I_3.
